content: A 1 cm segment of a wire lying along $x$-axis carries current of 0.5 A along $+x$ direction. A magnetic field $\vec{B}=(0.4 \mathrm{mT}) \hat{j}+(0.6 \mathrm{mT}) \hat{k}$ is switched on, in the region. The force acting on the segment is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { } I=0.5 \mathrm{~A}, l=1 \hat{i} \mathrm{~cm}=10^{-2} \hat{i}\,\,\, m, \\ & \vec{B}=(0.4 \mathrm{mT}) \hat{j}+(0.6 \mathrm{mT}) \hat{k} \\ & \vec{F}=I(\vec{l} \times \vec{B}) \\ & \vec{F}=0.5 \times 10^{-3}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 10^{-2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.4 & 0.6 \end{array}\right| \\ & \vec{F}=0.5 \times 10^{-3}\left[-\left(0.6 \times 10^{-2}\right) \hat{j}+\left(0.4 \times 10^{-2}\right) \hat{k}\right] \\ & \vec{F}=0.5(-6 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \mu \mathrm{N} \\ & \vec{F}=(-3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \mu \mathrm{N} \end{aligned} $$

content: You are required to design an air-filled solenoid of inductance 0.016 H having a length 0.81 m and radius 0.02 m . The number of turns in the solenoid should be
explanation:
Given

  • Inductance $ L = 0.016 \, \text{H} $

  • Length $ l = 0.81 \, \text{m} $

  • Radius $ r = 0.02 \, \text{m} $

  • Air core (μ = μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m)

We need the number of turns N.

Formula for inductance of a solenoid

$ L = \mu_0 \frac{N^2 A}{l} $

where

$ A = \pi r^2 $ is the cross-sectional area.

Rearranging for $ N $:

$ N = \sqrt{\frac{L \, l}{\mu_0 \, A}} $

Substitute the values

$ A = \pi (0.02)^2 = \pi \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2 $

$ \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} $

Now,

$ N^2 = \frac{L l}{\mu_0 A} = \frac{(0.016)(0.81)}{(1.2566\times10^{-6})(1.2566\times10^{-3})} $

Compute

$ L l = 0.01296 $

$ \mu_0 A = (1.2566\times10^{-6})(1.2566\times10^{-3}) = 1.579\times10^{-9} $

$ \frac{L l}{\mu_0 A} = \frac{0.01296}{1.579\times10^{-9}} \approx 8.21\times10^{6} $

Then,

$ N = \sqrt{8.21\times10^{6}} \approx 2866 $

✅ Answer: Option B — 2866 turns

content:
Assertion (A) : The deflection in a galvanometer is directly proportional to the current passing through it.

Reason (R) : The coil of a galvanometer is suspended in a uniform radial magnetic field.

options.content: Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason $(R)$ is the correct explanation of Assertion (A).
Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of Assertion (A).
Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
Assertion (A) is false and Reason (R) is also false.

explanation:
Assertion (A): The statement is true because, in a galvanometer, the angle the needle moves ($\theta$) changes in direct proportion to the amount of electric current ($I$) going through it.

$$\theta \propto I$$

The reason for this is that the force (called torque) that turns the coil depends on the current. So, if the current increases, the coil turns more.

Reason (R): This statement is also true.

The coil in a galvanometer moves inside a magnetic field that is even and spread out evenly in all directions (this is called a uniform radial magnetic field). This setup makes sure that the force on the coil depends only on the current, not on the coil's position.

Because the magnetic field is set up this way, the mathematical part called “sine of the angle between coil and field” always has the same value. This means the force (torque) stays directly linked to the current.

How Reason (R) explains Assertion (A):

The uniform radial magnetic field is the key reason the needle's movement $\theta$ and the current $I$ stay in step (in a straight-line relationship). That’s why the deflection follows the rule $\theta \propto I$.

content: A particle of mass $m$ and charge $q$ describes a circular path of radius $R$ in a magnetic field. If its mass and charge were $2 m$ and $\frac{q}{2}$ respectively, the radius of its path would be
explanation:
To determine the radius of the path when the mass and charge of the particle change, we first recognize that the magnetic force acting on a charged particle moving in a magnetic field is equal to the centripetal force, allowing the particle to move in a circular path.

The relationship is described as follows:

$ \text{Magnetic force} = \text{Centripetal force} $

Given by the equation:

$ qvB = \frac{mv^2}{R} $

Rearranging the terms, we can express the radius $ R $ as:

$ R = \frac{mv}{qB} $

Now, considering the new conditions, where the mass $ m $ changes to $ 2m $ and the charge $ q $ changes to $ \frac{q}{2} $:

Substitute $ m = 2m $ and $ q' = \frac{q}{2} $:

$ \frac{q}{2} B = \frac{2mv}{R'} $

    Solving for the new radius $ R' $:

    $ R' = \frac{4mv}{qB} $

    This shows that:

    $ R' = 4R $

    Therefore, under the new conditions where the mass is doubled and the charge is halved, the radius of the path becomes four times the original radius $ R $.

content:
Assertion (A): The energy of a charged particle moving in a magnetic field does not change.

Reason (R): It is because the work done by the magnetic force on the charge moving in a magnetic field is zero.

options.content: If both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is correct explanation of Assertion (A).
If both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of Assertion (A).
If Assertion (A) is true but Reason (R) is false.
If both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are false.

explanation: As the velocity and applied magnetic field are perpendicular to each other, hence, the work done by a magnetic force on a charge moving in a magnetic field is zero. This also means that the energy of the particle will not change. So, both the assertion and reason are true and reason correctly explains the assertion.

content: A long straight wire of radius '$$a$$' carries a steady current $$I$$. The current is uniformly distributed across its area of cross-section. The ratio of magnitude of magnetic field $$\vec{B}_1$$ at $$\frac{a}{2}$$ and $$\vec{B}_2$$ at distance $$2 a$$ is
explanation:
Magnetic field inside

$$B_1=\frac{\mu_0 i r}{2 \pi a^2}=\frac{\mu_0 i(a / 2)}{2 \pi a^2}=\frac{\mu_0 i}{4 \pi a}\quad$$ (Here, $$r=a/2$$)

Magnetic field outside

$$B_2=\frac{\mu_0 i}{2 \pi r}=\frac{\mu_0 i}{2 \pi(2 a)}=\frac{\mu_0 i}{4 \pi a}\quad$$ (Here, $$r=2a$$)

So, The required ratio $$\frac{B_1}{B_2}=1$$

content: Two long parallel wires kept $$2 \mathrm{~m}$$ apart carry $$3 \mathrm{~A}$$ current each, in the same direction. The force per unit length on one wire due to the other is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} i_1 & =i_2=3 \mathrm{~A} \\ r & =2 \mathrm{~m} \\ F / l & =\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \times \frac{i_2 i_2}{r} \end{aligned}$$

$$\left[\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi}=2 \times 10^{-7} N_A^{-2}\right]$$

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Or,} \quad & F / l=2 \times 10^{-7} \times \frac{3 \times 3}{2} \\ \therefore \quad & F / l=9 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned}$$

Force per unit length $$=9.0 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$$

Since, the currents are in same direction so there would be an attractive force between them.

content:
Assertion (A): The deflecting torque acting on a current carrying loop is zero when its plane is perpendicular to the direction of magnetic field.

Reason (R): The deflecting torque acting on a loop of magnetic moment $$\vec{m}$$ in a magnetic field $$\vec{B}$$ is given by the dot product of $$\vec{m}$$ and $$\vec{B}$$.

options.content: Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and (R) is NOT the correct explanation of (A).
Assertion (A) is true and Reason (R) is false.
Assertion (A) is false and Reason (R) is also false.

explanation:
$$\tau=N B I A \sin \theta$$

$$\theta$$ is the angle between the area vector and the direction of magnetic field.

If the plane of loop is perpendicular to the electric field, then the angle between the area vector and the direction electric field is $$0^0$$. Hence, $$\tau=0$$.

So, the assertion is true.

The torque is the cross product of the magnetic moment of the loop and the magnetic field. Hence the reason is false.

content: Two wires carrying currents I1 and I2 lie, one slightly above the other, in a horizontal plane as shown in figure. The region of vertically upward strongest magnetic field is
explanation: Applying right hand thumb rule, the strongest magnetic field will be in region II.

content: Two parallel conductors carrying current of 4.0 A and 10.0 A are placed 2.5 cm apart in vacuum. The force per unit length between them is -
explanation:
To find the force per unit length between two parallel conductors carrying currents, we use Ampère's force law. The formula for the force per unit length between two parallel wires separated by a distance $ d $ and carrying currents $ I_1 $ and $ I_2 $ is given by:

$$F/L = \frac{{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}}{{2\pi d}}$$

where:

  • $$F/L$$ is the force per unit length between the conductors
  • $$\mu_0$$ is the permeability of free space, which is $$4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{T} \cdot \text{m/A}$$ (tesla meter per ampere)
  • $$I_1$$ and $$I_2$$ are the currents carried by the conductors
  • $$d$$ is the distance between the conductors

Given values:

  • $$I_1 = 4.0 \, \text{A}$$
  • $$I_2 = 10.0 \, \text{A}$$
  • $$d = 2.5 \, \text{cm} = 0.025 \, \text{m}$$

Now substituting these values into the formula:

$$F/L = \frac{{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(4.0)(10.0)}}{{2\pi (0.025)}}$$

Let's simplify the expression:

$$F/L = \frac{{4 \times 10^{-7} \times 4.0 \times 10.0}}{{2 \times 0.025}}$$

$$F/L = \frac{{16 \times 10^{-7} \times 10}}{{0.05}}$$

$$F/L = \frac{{160 \times 10^{-7}}}{{0.05}}$$

$$F/L = 32 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{N/m}$$

Thus,

$$F/L = 3.2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{N/m}$$

Therefore, the closest option is:

Option D: 3.2 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$4 N/m

content: The magnetic field at the centre of a current carrying circular loop of radius R is B<sub>1</sub>. The magnetic field at a point on its axis at a distance R from the centre of the loop is B<sub>2</sub>. Then the ratio (B<sub>1</sub> / B<sub>2</sub>) is
explanation:
To find the ratio of the magnetic field at the center of a current-carrying circular loop ($$B_1$$) to the magnetic field on its axis at a distance $$R$$ from the center of the loop ($$B_2$$), we need to use the formulas for these fields.

The magnetic field at the center of the loop is given by:

$$B_1 = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R}$$

where $$\mu_0$$ is the permeability of free space, $$I$$ is the current, and $$R$$ is the radius of the loop.

The magnetic field at a point on the axis of the loop at a distance $$R$$ from the center is given by:

$$B_2 = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2 (R^2 + R^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2 (2R^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2 \cdot 2\sqrt{2} R^3} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \sqrt{2} R}$$

Now, we can find the ratio $$\frac{B_1}{B_2}$$:

$$\frac{B_1}{B_2} = \frac{\frac{\mu_0 I}{2R}}{\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \sqrt{2} R}} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R} \cdot \frac{4 \sqrt{2} R}{\mu_0 I} = \frac{4 \sqrt{2} R}{2R} = 2 \sqrt{2}$$

So, the ratio $$\frac{B_1}{B_2}$$ is $$2 \sqrt{2}$$.

The correct option is:

Option A $$2 \sqrt 2$$

content: A current carrying wire kept in a uniform magnetic field, will experience a maximum force when it is

options.content: at an angle of 45$$^\circ$$ to the magnetic field
at an angle of 60$$^\circ$$ to the magnetic field

explanation:
In physics, when a current-carrying conductor is placed in a magnetic field, it experiences a force. This force is given by the Lorentz force law, which states that the force $$\vec{F}$$ on a current-carrying wire in a magnetic field is given by:

$$ \vec{F} = I (\vec{L} \times \vec{B}) $$

Here, $$I$$ is the current, $$\vec{L}$$ is the length vector of the wire, and $$\vec{B}$$ is the magnetic field. The magnitude of this force is given by:

$$ F = I L B \sin \theta $$

where:

  • $$F$$ is the magnitude of the force
  • $$I$$ is the current flowing through the wire
  • $$L$$ is the length of the wire in the field
  • $$B$$ is the magnetic field strength
  • $$\theta$$ is the angle between the direction of the current and the magnetic field

To experience a maximum force, the value of $$\sin \theta$$ must be at its maximum, which is 1. This happens when $$\theta$$ = 90$$^\circ$$, i.e., when the wire is perpendicular to the magnetic field.

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option A: Perpendicular to the magnetic field

content: A straight conducting rod of length l and mass m is suspended in a horizontal plane by a pair of flexible strings in a magnetic field of magnitude B. To remove the tension in the supporting strings, the magnitude of the current in the wire is
explanation:
To determine the magnitude of the current required in the wire to remove the tension in the supporting strings, we need to analyze the forces acting on the rod in a magnetic field.

The rod experiences a gravitational force $ F_g $ due to its mass. The gravitational force can be written as:

$$ F_g = mg $$

where $ m $ is the mass of the rod and $ g $ is the acceleration due to gravity.

To counteract this gravitational force and remove the tension in the strings, a magnetic force $ F_B $ must act on the rod. The rod experiences a magnetic force when a current flows through it in the presence of a magnetic field. The magnetic force $ F_B $ on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field is given by:

$$ F_B = I l B $$

where $ I $ is the current flowing through the rod, $ l $ is the length of the rod, and $ B $ is the magnetic field strength.

To remove the tension in the strings, the magnetic force $ F_B $ must be equal to the gravitational force $ F_g $. Therefore, we have:

$$ mg = I l B $$

Solving for $ I $ (the current required), we get:

$$ I = \frac{mg}{lB} $$

Hence, the correct option is:

Option C

$$ I = \frac{mg}{lB} $$

content: A constant current is flowing through a solenoid. An iron rod is inserted in the solenoid along its axis. Which of the following quantities will not increase?

options.content: The magnetic field at the centre
The magnetic flux linked with the solenoid

explanation:
When an iron rod is inserted into a solenoid while a constant current is flowing through it, several quantities related to the solenoid and the magnetic field change. Let's analyze each option:

Option A: The magnetic field at the centre

Inserting an iron rod into the solenoid increases the magnetic permeability inside the solenoid. This enhances the magnetic field inside the solenoid because the magnetic field strength $ B $ in a solenoid with a core is given by:

$$ B = \mu \cdot (n \cdot I) $$

where $ \mu $ is the permeability of the core material (greater for iron), $ n $ is the number of turns per unit length, and $ I $ is the current. Thus, the magnetic field at the centre will increase.

Option B: The magnetic flux linked with the solenoid

The magnetic flux $ \Phi $ is given by:

$$ \Phi = B \cdot A $$

where $ B $ is the magnetic field and $ A $ is the cross-sectional area of the solenoid. Since $ B $ increases with the insertion of the iron rod, the magnetic flux linked with the solenoid also increases.

Option C: The rate of heating

The rate of heating in the solenoid depends on the power dissipated, which is given by:

$$ P = I^2 \cdot R $$

where $ I $ is the current and $ R $ is the resistance of the solenoid. The insertion of the iron rod does not directly affect the resistance $ R $ of the solenoid or the current $ I $, assuming a constant current. Therefore, the rate of heating remains unchanged.

Option D: The self-inductance of the solenoid

The self-inductance $ L $ of a solenoid with a core material is given by:

$$ L = \frac{\mu \cdot N^2 \cdot A}{l} $$

where $ \mu $ is the permeability of the core material, $ N $ is the total number of turns, $ A $ is the cross-sectional area, and $ l $ is the length of the solenoid. With the iron rod increasing the permeability $ \mu $, the self-inductance $ L $ will also increase.

Based on this analysis, the quantity that will not increase is:

Option C: The rate of heating

content: A proton and an alpha particle move in circular orbits in a uniform magnetic field. Their speeds are in the ratio of 9 : 4. The ratio of radii of their circular orbits $$\left( {{{{r_p}} \over {{r_{alpha}}}}} \right)$$ is
explanation:
To find the ratio of the radii of the circular orbits of a proton and an alpha particle in a uniform magnetic field, we need to consider the relationship between the radius of the orbit, the speed of the particle, the charge of the particle, and the mass of the particle.

The radius of the circular orbit in a magnetic field is given by the formula:

$$ r = \frac{mv}{qB} $$

where $ r $ is the radius, $ m $ is the mass of the particle, $ v $ is the speed of the particle, $ q $ is the charge of the particle, and $ B $ is the magnetic field strength.

For the proton ($ p $) and the alpha particle ($ \alpha $), we have the following expressions:

$$ r_p = \frac{m_p v_p}{q_p B} $$

$$ r_\alpha = \frac{m_\alpha v_\alpha}{q_\alpha B} $$

Given that the ratio of the speeds is $ \frac{v_p}{v_\alpha} = \frac{9}{4} $, we can express $ v_p $ and $ v_\alpha $ as $ v_p = 9k $ and $ v_\alpha = 4k $ where $ k $ is a constant.

The charge of a proton $ q_p $ is $ e $ and the mass of a proton $ m_p $ is $ m_p $. The alpha particle has a charge $ q_\alpha = 2e $ and a mass $ m_\alpha = 4m_p $.

Substituting these values into the radius equations, we get:

$$ r_p = \frac{m_p \cdot 9k}{e \cdot B} $$

$$ r_\alpha = \frac{4m_p \cdot 4k}{2e \cdot B} $$

Simplifying the expression for $ r_\alpha $:

$$ r_\alpha = \frac{16m_p \cdot k}{2e \cdot B} = \frac{8m_p \cdot k}{e \cdot B} $$

To find the ratio of the radii $ \left( \frac{r_p}{r_\alpha} \right) $, we divide $ r_p $ by $ r_\alpha $:

$$ \frac{r_p}{r_\alpha} = \frac{\frac{9m_p \cdot k}{e \cdot B}}{\frac{8m_p \cdot k}{e \cdot B}} = \frac{9}{8} $$

Therefore, the ratio of the radii of their circular orbits is:

$$ \frac{r_p}{r_\alpha} = \frac{9}{8} $$

Hence, the correct option is:

$$ \text{Option D:} \frac{9}{8} $$

content:
Assertion (A) : When radius of a current carrying loop is doubled, its magnetic moment becomes four times.

Reason (R) : The magnetic moment of a current carrying loop is directly proportional to the area of the loop.

options.content: Both (A) & (R) are true and (R) is correct explanation of (A).
Both (A) & (R) are true and (R) is not correct explanation of (A).
(A) is false and (R) is also false.

explanation:
To understand the given assertions and reason, let's examine the magnetic moment of a current carrying loop in detail.

The magnetic moment ($ \mu $) of a current carrying loop is given by the formula:

$$ \mu = I \cdot A $$

where $ I $ is the current and $ A $ is the area of the loop.

For a circular loop, the area $ A $ is:

$$ A = \pi r^2 $$

where $ r $ is the radius of the loop.

Therefore, the magnetic moment of a circular loop can be expressed as:

$$ \mu = I \cdot \pi r^2 $$

Now, let's analyze the assertion and reason:

Assertion (A): When the radius of a current carrying loop is doubled, its magnetic moment becomes four times.

If the radius $ r $ is doubled, the new radius will be $ 2r $. The new area $ A' $ will be:

$$ A' = \pi (2r)^2 = 4 \pi r^2 $$

The new magnetic moment $ \mu' $ is:

$$ \mu' = I \cdot A' = I \cdot 4 \pi r^2 = 4 \cdot (I \cdot \pi r^2) = 4 \mu $$

Therefore, we can see that when the radius is doubled, the magnetic moment becomes four times, so Assertion (A) is true.

Reason (R): The magnetic moment of a current carrying loop is directly proportional to the area of the loop.

From the formula $ \mu = I \cdot A $, it is evident that the magnetic moment is directly proportional to the area of the loop. Hence, Reason (R) is also true.

Moreover, the reason (R) correctly explains why the magnetic moment becomes four times when the radius is doubled because the area increases by a factor of four. Therefore, the correct option is:

Option A: Both (A) & (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).

content: Using Biot-Savart law, derive expression for the magnetic field $(B)$ due to a circular current carrying loop at a point on its axis and hence at its centre.
explanation:
Let us consider a circular loop of radius $a$ with centre C. Let the plane of the coil be perpendicular to plane of the paper and current $I$ be flowing in the direction shown. Suppose $P$ is any point on the axis a distance $r$ form the centre.

Let us consider a current element $d l$ on top $(L)$ where, current comes out of paper normally whereas at bottom ( $M$ ) enters into the plane paper normally.

$$ \begin{aligned} & L P \perp d l \\ & M P \perp d l \\ & L P=M P=\sqrt{r^2+a^2} \end{aligned} $$

Now, magnetic field at $P$ due to current element at $L$ according to Biot-Savart Law,

$$ d B=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I d l \sin 90^{\circ}}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)} $$

where, $\quad a=$ radius of circular loop

$r=$ distance of point $P$ from centre along the axis. $d B \cos \phi$ components balance each other and net magnetic field is given by

$$ \begin{aligned} B & =\oint d B \sin \phi \\ & =\oint \frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi}\left[\frac{I d l}{r^2+a^2}\right] \cdot \frac{a}{\sqrt{r^2+a^2}} \\ & {\left[\therefore \operatorname{In} \Delta P C M \sin \phi=\frac{a}{\sqrt{r^2+a^2}}\right] } \\ & =\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I a}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \int d l \\ & =\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I a}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \times 2 \pi a \end{aligned} $$

$$ B=\frac{\mu_0 I a^2}{2\left(r^2+a^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} $$

As the direction of the field is along $+v e \mathrm{X}$-direction, we can write

$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_0 I a^2}{2\left(r^2+a^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i} $$

$$ \begin{aligned}At \,\,its \,\,\,centre,\,\,\, & r=0 \\ & B=\frac{\mu_o I a^2}{2\left(a^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 a} \end{aligned} $$

content:
(a) (i) A proton moving with velocity $\vec{V}$ in a non. uniform magnetic field traces a path as shown in the figure.

The path followed by the proton is always in the plane of the paper. What is the direction of the magnetic field in the region near points $P, Q$ and $R$ ? What can you say about relative magnitude of magnetic fields at these points?

(ii) A current carrying circular loop of area $A$ produces a magnetic field $B$ at its centre. Show that the magnetic moment of the loop is $\frac{2 B A}{\mu_0} \sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}}$

OR

(b) (i) Derive an expression for the torque acting on a rectangular current loop suspended in a uniform magnetic field.

(ii) A charged particle is moving in a circular path with velocity $\vec{V}$ in a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$. It is made to pass through a sheet of lead and as a consequence, it looses one half of its kinetic energy without change in its direction. How will (1) the radius of its path (2) its time period of revolution change?
explanation:
(a) (i) A proton is a positively charged particle, and its trajectory is influenced by the Lorentz force in a magnetic field. The force on a charged particle in a magnetic field is given by:

$$ \vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) $$

Since, the force is always perpendicular to the velocity, the particle follows a curved path. The nature of curvature indicates the direction and variation in the magnetic field.

The proton follows a curved trajectory that remains in the plane of the paper.

This suggests that the force acting on the proton is perpendicular to its velocity in the plane.

Since, the proton curves upward, the force must be towards the centre of curvature, meaning the magnetic field is into the plane of the paper at all points using right-hand rule.

Thus, at points $P, Q$, and $R$, the magnetic field is directed into the paper.

The radius of curvature $r$ of a charged particle's motion in a magnetic field is given by:

$$ r=\frac{m v}{q B} $$

If the radius is larger, the magnetic field is weaker. If the radius is smaller, the magnetic field is stronger.

Observing the trajectory:

  • At $P$, the path is relatively straight, meaning $B$ is smaller.

  • At $Q$, the curvature increases, meaning $B$ is increasing.

  • At $R$, the curvature is highest, meaning $B$ is largest.

Thus, the relative magnitude follows: $B_P

(ii) The magnetic moment $(\mu)$ of a current-carrying circular loop is given by:

$$ \mu=I A $$

The magnetic field at the centre of a circular loop carrying current is given by:

$$ B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R} $$

The area of the loop is:

$$ A=\pi R^2 \Rightarrow R=\sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}} $$

Now, substitute $R$ back into the equation for B :

$$ B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}}} $$

This simplifies to: $B=\frac{\mu_0 I \sqrt{\pi}}{2 \sqrt{A}}$

Rearranging the equation to solve for $I$ :

$$ I=\frac{2 B \sqrt{A}}{\mu_0 \sqrt{\pi}} $$

The magnetic moment $m$ of the loop is given by:

$$ m=I . A $$

Substituting the expression for $I$ :

$$ m=\left(\frac{2 B \sqrt{A}}{\mu_0 \sqrt{\pi}}\right) \cdot A $$

This simplifies to: $m=\frac{2 B A \sqrt{A}}{\mu_0 \sqrt{\pi}}$

OR

(b) (i) Consider the simple case when a rectangular loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field $B$ that is in the plane of the loop.

Force on arm $A B=F_1=I b B$ (directed into the plane of the loop)

Force on arm $C D=F_2=I b B$ (directed outward the plane of the loop)

Therefore, the magnitude of the torque on the loop due to these pair of forces is

$$ \begin{aligned} \tau & =F_1 \frac{a}{2}+F_2 \frac{a}{2} \\ & =I(a b) B \\ & =I A B=m B \\ (A & =a b=\text { area of the loop }) \end{aligned} $$

Alternatively,

Also, accept if the student obtains the result.

$$ \text { Torque }=I A B \sin \theta $$

(ii) (1) The radius of a charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field is given by:

$$ r=\frac{m v}{q B} $$

The initial kinetic energy of the particle:

$$ K_i=\frac{1}{2} m v_i^2 $$

After losing half of its kinetic energy:

$$ \begin{aligned} K_f\left(\frac{1}{2} m v_f^2\right) & =\frac{1}{2} K_i=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2} m v_i^2\right) \\ v_f & =\frac{v_i}{\sqrt{2}} \\ r & =\frac{m v}{q B} \\ r_f & =\frac{m v_f}{q B} \\ & =\frac{m v_i}{q B \sqrt{2}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} r_i \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the radius decreases by a factor of $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.

(2) The time period of revolution for a charged particle in a magnetic field is given by:

$$ T=\frac{2 \pi m}{q B} $$

Since, the time period does not depend on velocity, it remains unchanged even though the kinetic energy is reduced.

content: Derive an expression for magnetic force $\vec{F}$ acting on a straight conductor of length $L$ carrying current I in an external magnetic field $\vec{B}$. Is it valid when the conductor is in zig-zag form? Justify.
explanation:
Since, the force on an individual charge moving at the drift velocity $v_d$

$$F=q v_d B \sin \theta$$

For N charge carriers

$$\begin{aligned} F & =\left(q v_d B \sin \theta\right)(N) \\ \text{where,}\quad N & =n V=n A L \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad F & =q v_d B \sin \theta n A L \\ F & =n q A v_d B \sin \theta L \\ F & =I L B \sin \theta \quad\left[\because I=n q A v_d\right] \\ \vec{F} & =I(\vec{L} \times \vec{B}) \end{aligned}$$

Yes, it is valid even when the conductor is in zig-zag form as then also the length of the wire will remain at the same angle with the magnetic field as before.

content: An electron moving with a velocity $\vec{v}=\left(1.0 \times 10^7\right.$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}) \hat{i}+\left(0.5 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right) \hat{j}$ enters a region of uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}=(0.5 \mathrm{mT}) \hat{j}$. Find the radius of the circular path described by it. While rotating; does the electron trace a linear path too? If so, calculate the linear distance covered by it during the period of one revolution.
explanation:
Given, $\quad \begin{aligned} \vec{v} & =\left(1.0 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right) \hat{i}+\left(0.5 \times 10^7 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right) \hat{j} \\ \vec{B} & =(0.5 \mathrm{mT}) \hat{j}\end{aligned}$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Since, } \quad \frac{m v^2}{r}=q v B\\ &\Rightarrow \quad r=\frac{m v_x}{q B}=\frac{\left(9.10 \times 10^{-31} \times 1.0 \times 10^7\right)}{\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\right)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} r & =11.3875 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m} \\ r & =11.39 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$$

Now,

$$\begin{aligned} & T=\frac{2 \pi r}{v}=\frac{2 \pi m}{q B} \\ & T=\frac{2 \times 3.14 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}} \\ & T=71.51 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~s} \\ & T=71.51 \mathrm{~ns} \end{aligned}$$

The electron traces a linear path too. For one revolution pitch,

$$\begin{aligned} T \times v_y & =71.51 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.5 \times 10^7 \\ & =35.75 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m} \\ & =35.75 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$$

content:
(a) Write the expression for the Lorentz force on a particle of charge $$q$$ moving with a velocity $$\vec{v}$$ in a magnetic field $$\vec{B}$$. When is the magnitude of this force maximum? Show that no work is done by this force on the particle during its motion from a point $$\vec{r_1} \text { to point } \vec{r}_2 \text {. }$$

OR

(b) A long straight wire $$A B$$ carries a current I. A particle (mass $$m$$ and charge $$q$$ ) moves with a velocity $$\vec{v}$$, parallel to the wire, at a distance $$d$$ from it as shown in the figure. Obtain the expression for the force experienced by the particle and mention its directions.
explanation:
(a) Expression for Lorentz force:

$$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

The force is maximum when the angle between $$\vec{v}$$ and $$\vec{B}$$ is $$90^0$$.

Here, $$\vec{\mathrm{F}}$$ is perpendicular to $$\vec{v}$$. So, no work is done by this force on the particle during its motion

OR

(b) Magnetic field produced by the current carrying from a point $$\vec{r}_1$$ to point $$\vec{r}_2$$ wire, $$B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi d}$$

The direction of field is $$\otimes$$

Force acting on the particle $$=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

Here, $$\theta=90^{\circ}$$

So, Force $$=q v B=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{q v}{d} \mathrm{I}$$

Its direction is towards right. Repulsive.
